title: Hybrid Cloud/Local with Claude Code
description: How to route Claude Code between Anthropic's cloud (Max/Pro) and local models, using subagents as the routing boundary.

stops.title: The Goal: Seamless Hybrid
stops.narrative:
[Claude Code](node:claude-code) speaks only the [Anthropic Messages API](node:anthropic-api). The goal is to run the main orchestrator on Anthropic's cloud ([Max](node:claude-max) or [Pro](node:claude-pro)) for complex reasoning, while routing [subagents](node:subagent) to local models for tasks that don't need cloud-grade intelligence.

This saves cloud tokens, reduces latency for simple operations, works offline for exploration, and keeps your subscription usage within limits.

stops.title: The Cloud Side: Max vs Pro
stops.narrative:
[Claude Max](node:claude-max) ($100-200/month) gives 20x more Claude Code usage than [Pro](node:claude-pro) ($20/month). In a hybrid setup, the subscription tier determines how aggressively you need to offload to local.

**Max plan**: comfortable headroom — route subagents locally as an optimization, not a necessity. Use cloud for everything complex.

**Pro plan**: tighter budget — local routing becomes essential. Reserve cloud tokens for the main session's planning, multi-file edits, and architectural decisions. Route all exploration, search, and simple tasks locally.

Both plans give you Opus and Sonnet. The main Claude Code session — the one that understands your full request, plans the approach, and orchestrates subagents — should always run on cloud.

stops.title: Subagents: The Routing Boundary
stops.narrative:
[Subagents](node:subagent) are the natural split point. Claude Code launches them for specific subtasks — each is an independent LLM call with its own context. This makes them perfect routing candidates.

**Send to local** (a 32B model handles these fine):
- **Explore/search agents**: grep for patterns, find files, read directory structures
- **Code search**: "find where this function is defined", "list all API endpoints"
- **Simple file reads**: summarize a file, extract a specific section
- **Test runners**: run tests and report pass/fail (the LLM just interprets output)
- **Linting/formatting**: check style, report issues
- **Git operations**: parse git log, diff summary, branch status

**Keep on cloud** (needs Opus/Sonnet reasoning):
- **Multi-file edits**: coordinated changes across several files
- **Architectural planning**: design decisions, migration strategies
- **Complex refactoring**: rename across codebase, restructure modules
- **Bug diagnosis**: tracing through complex call chains
- **Code generation**: writing new features from specs
- **The main orchestrator session itself**

stops.title: Routing Strategy in Practice
stops.narrative:
The [task routing](node:task-routing) decision comes down to: does this task need deep reasoning or just pattern matching?

A local [Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B](node:qwen25-coder-32b) or [Devstral Small 2](node:devstral-small-2) can reliably:
- Parse tool call requests (grep, read, glob)
- Summarize file contents
- Answer "where is X defined" questions
- Run a command and interpret the output

It will struggle with:
- Holding 50+ files in context while planning edits
- Understanding nuanced architectural constraints
- Generating correct code for complex interfaces
- Multi-step reasoning chains

The heuristic: if a subagent's job is mostly **reading and reporting**, it goes local. If it's **deciding and writing**, it stays on cloud.

stops.title: CCProxy: The Hybrid Router
stops.narrative:
[CCProxy](node:ccproxy) is the most practical way to implement this. It sits between Claude Code and the backends, inspecting each request to decide routing.

CCProxy can detect subagent requests (shorter context, specific tool patterns) and route them to local [Ollama](node:ollama), while forwarding the main session to Anthropic's cloud via your [Max](node:claude-max)/[Pro](node:claude-pro) subscription.

It also supports OAuth for Claude Pro/Max — so your main session authenticates against Anthropic normally, while subagents hit localhost.

This is the recommended setup for serious hybrid usage.

stops.title: Path 1: Direct Ollama (All-Local)
stops.narrative:
The simplest setup. Since [Ollama](node:ollama) v0.14.0 supports the [Anthropic API](node:anthropic-api) natively:

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL="http://localhost:11434"
export ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN="dummy"
claude
```

This sends everything to local models. Use `/model` inside Claude Code to switch between models you've pulled. Limitation: no automatic cloud fallback for complex tasks — you manually switch.

Claude Code sends a ~16k token system prompt, so you need models with at least 64K context.

Good for: offline work, exploration sessions, simple edits where you don't want to burn cloud tokens.

stops.title: Path 3: Full Proxy with LiteLLM
stops.narrative:
[LiteLLM](node:litellm) is the general-purpose option. It translates between the [Anthropic API](node:anthropic-api) and [OpenAI-compatible](node:openai-api) backends, adds load balancing, fallbacks, and cost tracking.

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL="http://0.0.0.0:4000"
export ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN="$LITELLM_MASTER_KEY"
```

More complex to set up than [CCProxy](node:ccproxy) but more flexible — works with any backend, not just Claude Code. You can configure routing rules that send requests under a token threshold to local and everything else to Anthropic.

Security note: vet your LiteLLM version carefully (past PyPI compromise).

stops.title: Alternative: Aider for Local-Only Work
stops.narrative:
If you want a coding assistant that's fully local-native, [Aider](node:aider) speaks [OpenAI-compatible API](node:openai-api) directly. No proxy needed — point it at [Ollama](node:ollama), [llama.cpp](node:llama-cpp) server, or [LM Studio](node:lm-studio).

Aider has deep git integration, multi-file editing, and auto-commits. Use it alongside Claude Code: Aider for local tasks, Claude Code for cloud tasks. Or use [Continue.dev](node:continue-dev) in your IDE for inline completions from a local model while Claude Code handles complex work in the terminal.

stops.title: The Protocol Bridge
stops.narrative:
Everything hinges on two protocols: the [Anthropic Messages API](node:anthropic-api) (/v1/messages) and the [OpenAI-compatible API](node:openai-api) (/v1/chat/completions).

Claude Code only speaks Anthropic. Most local engines only speak OpenAI. The bridge between them — [Ollama](node:ollama) (native), [CCProxy](node:ccproxy), or [LiteLLM](node:litellm) — is the critical piece.

Tool calling (function calling) is the main pain point. Some local engines drop tool call responses during streaming, limiting models to chat-only interactions. [Qwen3-Coder-Next](node:qwen3-coder-next) and [Devstral 2](node:devstral-2) have the best tool calling support among local models.

stops.title: Recommended Local Models for Subagents
stops.narrative:
For local subagent work, the model choice depends on the task:

**Exploration/search subagents** — [Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B](node:qwen25-coder-32b) at Q8_0 (~34GB). Reliable tool calling, 128K context, fast enough for grep-and-report tasks. The workhorse.

**Agentic tasks with tool use** — [Devstral Small 2](node:devstral-small-2) (24B). Purpose-built for agentic coding. Strong tool calling. Lighter than Qwen 32B.

**Fast secondary** — [Phi-4](node:phi-4) (14B) at Q8_0 (~15GB). For trivial tasks like parsing command output or summarizing short files. Can run alongside a larger model.

**If you have headroom** — [Qwen3-Coder-Next](node:qwen3-coder-next) (80B MoE, only 3B active). Near-Sonnet quality on coding. If it fits, it's the best local option for subagents that need more intelligence.
